meta.name: GitHub Foundations (GH-900)
meta.description: Core GitHub concepts for the GH-900 certification — repositories, branches, pull requests, issues, Actions, security, and collaboration.

cards.definition: A cloud-based platform for version control, collaboration, and DevOps workflows built around Git.
cards.hint: The home for modern software development.

cards.definition: A distributed version control system that tracks changes in source code and enables collaboration.
A storage location for code, documentation, and project files. Can be public or private.
A parallel version of the codebase used to develop features or fixes without affecting the main branch.
A snapshot of changes in a repository, including a message describing what changed.
A request to merge changes from one branch into another. Supports code review, comments, and approvals.
Combining changes from one branch into another, usually via a pull request.
A personal copy of someone else's repository used to propose changes or build independently.
cards.hint: Your own copy of a repo.

cards.definition: A local copy of a GitHub repository created on your machine.
cards.hint: Download the repo to your computer.

cards.definition: Track bugs, tasks, enhancements, and discussions. Can be assigned, labeled, and linked to PRs.
Tags applied to issues and PRs to categorize and prioritize work.
Group issues and PRs toward a specific goal or release.
Kanban-style boards for planning and tracking work across repos.
A markdown file that introduces and documents a project. Usually the first file users see.
A lightweight markup language used for formatting text in GitHub files like README.md.
GitHub's CI/CD automation platform used to build, test, and deploy code.
A YAML-defined automation process triggered by events such as pushes, PRs, or schedules.
A machine that executes GitHub Actions workflows. GitHub-hosted or self-hosted.
Encrypted values (API keys, tokens) used securely in workflows.
A package registry for storing and sharing container images, npm packages, NuGet, and more.
A cloud-based development environment with VS Code running in the browser.
An AI coding assistant that suggests code, generates functions, and accelerates development.
Notifications about vulnerabilities in dependencies or code.
A GitHub tool that automatically updates dependencies and alerts you to vulnerabilities.
Analyzes code for security vulnerabilities using tools like CodeQL.
Detects accidentally committed secrets like API keys.
Policies that enforce requirements like PR reviews, status checks, and no-force-push.
A shared account for managing multiple repositories, teams, and permissions.
Groups of users within an organization used to manage permissions and collaboration.
Access levels such as read, write, and admin applied to repos and orgs.
A static site hosting service for documentation or project websites.
cards.hint: Host a website from your repo.

cards.definition: The process of reviewing code changes, leaving comments, and approving or requesting changes.
A situation where Git cannot automatically merge changes because the same lines were modified differently.
A forum-like space for community conversations, Q&A, and long-form collaboration.
Predefined issue or PR templates that standardize contributions.
A catalog of reusable GitHub Actions created by the community and vendors.
A human-readable format used to define GitHub Actions workflows.
Software whose source code is publicly available for use, modification, and contribution.
Specifies how others can use, modify, and distribute your code.
Documentation that explains how to contribute to a project.
A command-line tool for interacting with GitHub directly from the terminal.
cards.hint: Use GitHub from the command line.

cards.definition: Automatically scales self-hosted runners for large CI/CD workloads.
Specify individuals or teams responsible for reviewing changes to specific files or directories.
Analytics for repository activity, contributions, and project health.
Premium security features including code scanning, secret scanning, and dependency review.
Shows all dependencies used in a repository and highlights vulnerabilities.
cards.hint: See what your project relies on.

cards.definition: Files generated by workflows that can be stored and downloaded.
Allows programmatic access to GitHub resources such as repos, issues, and workflows.
A flexible API for querying GitHub data with precise control over returned fields.
A set of practices that combine development and operations to deliver software faster and more reliably, supported by GitHub tools.
